questions.text: Used in rechargeable batteries, this light element is also found in many rocks. Can you identify it?

Propane (C<sub>3</sub>H<sub>8</sub>) reacts with high pressure steam (H<sub>2</sub>O) to make carbon monoxide (CO) and hydrogen (H<sub>2</sub>). Write the balanced reaction for this process and calculate the number of grams of propane required to produce #a# moles of H<sub>2</sub>.
questions.solution:
The balanced reaction is
C3H8 + 3 H2O → 3 CO + 7 H2
Starting point: #a# mol H2
Goal: g propane
Plan: mol H2 → mol propane → g propane
Molar mass propane = 44.1 g/mol
#a# mol H2 * (|1 mol propane|7 mol H2|) * (|44.1 g propane|1 mol propane|) = #a/7*44.1# g propane

questions.text:
Calculate the change in enthalpy (ΔH) for the reaction
C3H8 + 3 H2O → 3 CO + 7 H2
The enthalpies of formation are
ΔHf[C3H8] = -103.8 kJ/mol
ΔHf[H2O] = -241.82 kJ/mol
ΔHf[CO] = -110.52 kJ/mol
ΔHf[H2] = 0 kJ/mol
How much heat input is required to generate #a# moles of H2?
questions.solution:
ΔHrxn = ΔHf(products) - ΔHf(reactants) = [3*(-110.52) + 7*0] - [-103.8 + 3*(-241.82)] = +497.7 kJ/mol
#a# mol H2 * (|497.7 kJ|7 mol H2|) = #a*497.7/7# kJ

questions.text:
Balance the following combustion reaction to determine the stoichiometric coefficient for oxygen. In other words, what is the value of x in the equation below?
C#a+2*b#H#4*(b+c)#O2 + x O2 → ? CO2 + ? H2O
questions.solution:
Each mole of C#a+2*b#H#4*(b+c)#O2 produces #a+2*b# moles of CO2 and #2*(b+c)# moles of H2O
This gives the partially balanced reaction:
C#a+2*b#H#4*(b+c)#O2 + x O2 → #a+2*b# CO2 + #2*(b+c)# H2O
There are #2*(a+2*b)+2*(b+c)# moles of O atoms on the products side, and 2 moles of O atoms on the reactants side (in the fuel), so we need to provide #2*(a+2*b)+2*(b+c)-2# moles of O atoms or #a+2*b+b+c-1# moles of O2 on the reactant side to balance the reaction:
C#a+2*b#H#4*(b+c)#O2 + #a+2*b+b+c-1# O2 → #a+2*b# CO2 + #2*(b+c)# H2O

questions.text: John Dalton's Law of
questions.tag: Proportions states that if two elements form more than one compound between them, then the ratios of the masses of the second element which combine with a fixed mass of the first element will be ratios of small whole numbers. This is the one of the most basic laws of stoichiometry.

If a sample of unknown substance has a mass of #a/10# kg and a volume of #b/10# L, what is the density of the substance when expressed in g/cm<sup>3</sup>?
questions.solution:
#a/10# kg = #a*100# g
#b/10# L = #b*100# cm3

questions.text: If a metal alloy has a density of #a/100# g/cm<sup>3</sup>, what is the volume of a #b/100# kg sample of the metal?
questions.solution:
First convert kg to g: mass = #b/100# kg = #b*10# g
The volume of the sample is given by mass divided by density:
V = (#b*10# g) / (#a/100# g/cm3) = #b*10/(a/100)# cm3

questions.text: If a sample of unknown substance has a mass of #a/10# kg and a volume of #b/10# L, what is the density of the substance when expressed in conventional (but non-SI) units of g/cm<sup>3</sup>?
questions.solution:
#a/10# kg = #100*a# g
#b/10# L = #b*100# cm3
density = (#100*a# g) / (#b*100# cm3) = #a/b# g/cm3

questions.solution: The mass of two H atoms is 2 amu, and the mass of one O atom is 16 amu, Therefore, the mass ratio O:H in water is 8:1 and in hydrogen peroxide is 16:1.  To make hydrogen peroxide with #b# g of hydrogen requires 16 * #b# = #16*b# g of oxygen.

questions.text: The number of carbon atoms in the formula unit for calcium cyanide is
How much water must be added to #10*a# mL of a stock solution of #0.1*c*b# M NaCl to prepare a #0.1*b# M solution of NaCl?
questions.solution:
The number of moles of NaCl in #a*10# mL of stock solution is
#0.1*c*b# mol/L x #10*a# mL / (1000 mL/L) = #0.1*c*b*10*a/1000# mol NaCl
The total volume of solution required to prepare a #0.1*b# M solution using this amount of NaCl is #0.1*c*b*10*a/1000# mol / (#0.1*b# M) = #c*10*a/1000# L or #c*10*a# mL
Therefore, the extra volume of water required is #c*10*a# mL - #10*a# mL = #10*a*c-10*a# mL

questions.text: A #a# mL sample of water from the Chesapeake Bay was found to contain #b/100# g of sodium chloride. What is the salt concentration expressed in ppm by mass?

conc. NaCl = (|#b/100# g NaCl|#a# mL H2O|) * (|1 mL H2O|1 g H2O|) * (|1 ppm NaCl * 106 g H2O|1 g NaCl|) = #b/100/a*1e6# ppm NaCl

Fe2O3(s) + 3 CO(g) → 2 Fe(s) + 3 CO2(g)

Ammonia (NH<sub>3</sub>) is synthesized from nitrogen (N<sub>2</sub>) and hydrogen (H<sub>2</sub>) by mixing the reactants in the presence of a metal catalyst at high temperature and high pressure. How many moles of H<sub>2</sub> are required to make #a/10# moles of ammonia?
questions.solution:
The balanced reaction is:
N2 + 3 H2 → 2 NH3
3 mol H2 = 2 mol NH3
moles H2 needed = #a/10# mol NH3 * (|3 mol H2|2 mol NH3|) = #a/10*3/2# mol H2

BaCl2(s) + 2 Li(s) → Ba(s) + 2 LiCl(s)

P + 5/2 Cl2 → PCl5
#a/10# mol of P is required to make #a/10# mol of PCl5, and the mass of reactant is
#a/10# mol * 30.97 g/mol = #a/10*30.97# g

questions.text: A solution that has a pH of less than 7 is considered ________.

A solution containing #a/1000# moles of hydrochloric acid was titrated with a solution of sodium hydroxide to the endpoint, which occurred at #b/10# mL added base.
NaOH(aq) + HCl(aq) → NaCl(aq) + H2O(l)
What was the concentration of the original NaOH solution?
questions.solution:
Starting point: (|#a/1000# mol HCl|#b/10# mL soln|)
Goal: (|mol NaOH|1 L soln|)
(|#a/1000# mol HCl|#b/10# mL NaOH|) * (|1 mL|10-3 L|) * (|1 mol NaOH|1 mol HCl|) = #a/1000/(b/10)/1e-3# (|mol NaOH|1 L soln|) = #a/1000/(b/10)/1e-3# M NaOH
questions.hints: The concentration of the original NaOH solution is equal to the number moles of acid neutralized divided by the volume of solution added in the titration.

questions.text: When #a/100# g of silver nitrate is added to a solution containing excess NaCl(aq), a precipitate of AgCl(s) is collected, washed and dried. The mass of the precipitate is #a/100*143.32/169.87*b/1000# g. What is the percent yield for the reaction?
questions.solution:
Theoretical yield:
#a/100# g AgNO3 * (|1 mol AgNO3|169.87 g AgNO3|) * (|1 mol AgCl|1 mol AgNO3|) * (|143.32 g AgCl|1 mol AgCl|) = #a/100/169.87*143.32# g AgCl
Percent yield = (|observed yield|theoretical yield|) * 100% = (|#a/100*143.32/169.87*b/1000# g AgCl|#a/100/169.87*143.32# g AgCl|) * 100% = #b/1000*100#% yield

questions.text: If the specific heat capacity of a metal is #a*10# J/(kg-K), what is the predicted change in temperature when #b# J of heat is added to a #c/100# kg sample of the metal?
questions.solution:
The predicted temperature rise is directly proportional to the amount of heat added and inversely proportional to the sample mass and specific heat capacity:
ΔT = (#b# J) / (#c/100# kg) / (#a*10# J/(kg-K)) = #b/(c/100)/(a*10)# K

questions.solution: The outer N atom is surrounded by 8 electrons (4 electrons in the double bond, plus 2 lone pairs). Its formal charge is equal to 5 (the original number of valence electrons on N) minus 6 (2 lone pairs plus half the number of electrons in the double bond).  Formal charge = 5-6 = -1.

questions.text: In the Lewis resonance structure for carbon dioxide (CO<sub>2</sub>) in which the central C atom is doubly bonded to each of the two O atoms, what is the formal charge on the central C atom?

Estimate the change in enthalpy for synthesis of 1 mole of ammonia (NH3) from N2 and H2.
Average bond energies are H-H (436 kJ/mol), N≡N (946 kJ/mol), and H-N (390 kJ/mol).
questions.solution:
For every 2 moles of NH3 formed, we must break 3 H-H bonds and 1 N≡N bond, but we also form 6 H-N bonds:
Change in enthalpy = 3*436 + 946 - 6*390 = -86 kJ/mol
Then for every 1 mol of NH3 formed, ΔH = -86/2 = -43 kJ/mol
